lines.text: Maya's table, three trays deep in fries and gossip. Jordan's mid-story about something that happened in gym. At the far end of the next table, Alex sits alone, notebook open, pencil moving.
Okay but real talk—
Wren. Two weeks in. You've been holding out on us. What's the tea?
Oh, here we go—
I'm serious! Everybody's got a thing. Jordan's thing is being nice to literally everyone, which — boring—
Hey—
—so what's yours?
Okay, um — okay, you know Alex? From third period?
lines.action: glances toward Alex's table, chews the inside of her cheek

lines.text: The quiet one with the notebook.
Yeah, so — I sat next to them for like, four minutes during that group thing, and I saw—
lines.action: leans in, feels the table lean with her

lines.text: —there's a whole kingdom in there. Like a map. Provinces. A flag.
Wait, what—
A flag—?
And it has a name. A real, whole, official name.
The Doodle Kingdom.
The WHAT—
Okay that's — okay that's actually kind of—
lines.emotion: laughing harder than the joke earns

lines.text: Presenting His Royal Highness, ruler of several margins and one (1) trapper keeper—
lines.action: pitches into a mock-royal announcer voice, riding the laugh

lines.text: Stop, oh my god, STOP—
lines.action: wheezing, glancing around the table to check who's laughing

lines.text: Twelve feet away, at the end of the next table, Alex has gone very still.
The pencil stops moving.
Alex closes the notebook — slow, careful, like it might still be listening — and stands.
No slammed tray. No look back. Just the sound of a chair pushed in, and the cafeteria door swinging shut.
The door is still swinging. Wren's laugh is still half in her mouth.
...That was probably just a bathroom thing. Right? People just — leave.
lines.emotion: smile faltering half a beat too late

lines.text: Anyway — Jordan, finish the gym story, you can't just leave us at 'and then the ball—'
lines.action: already reaching for Jordan's fries, not looking at the door

lines.text: Right, um — so the ball—
lines.action: glances at Wren, then the door, then back

lines.text: (Her stomach is doing something complicated and not funny at all.)
...That was kind of loud, huh. What I said.
Relax, it's fine, they'll get over it. Jordan. The BALL.
The door doesn't open again.
The hallway's mostly empty — the kind of quiet that makes footsteps sound like an accusation.
Alex is at the lockers, notebook hugged to their chest, not looking up.
Hey — hey, wait, can I—
It's fine.
It's really not, though. That's — that's why I'm here.
...Okay.
A beat. Alex's fingers tap the edge of the notebook — four taps, always four.
Wren tells herself she'll catch Alex after class. There's time. There's always time, right up until there isn't.
The bell rings before she's decided anything at all.
Through the crowd, she catches a glimpse — Alex, spine very straight, notebook clutched tight, slipping toward Mr. Alvarez's room.
(The door's still open. Barely.)
Mr. Alvarez leans against the front desk, clipboard in hand like it's a formality he doesn't need.
Identity Map project. Pairs. And before anyone asks — no, you don't get to pick, and no, that's not up for negotiation.
He's smiling like he's about to ruin somebody's week.
Please not me and Jordan again, I cannot survive his Google Slides transitions—
Noted, Maya. Considered. Overruled.
...Maya, Jordan.
Yes—
...Wren. Alex.
Alex's shoulders go rigid — then, slowly, not all the way back down, but not locked either.
Hey.
...Hey.
lines.action: taps the desk once — not four, just one

lines.text: Details are on the board. Get weird with it, people.
lines.action: glance lingers half a second too long on the two of them

lines.text: Mr. Alvarez leans against the front desk, clipboard in hand.
Identity Map project. Pairs. Not your choice, not up for negotiation.
...Maya, Jordan.
Yes—
...Wren. Alex.
Alex's face closes like a door — fast, complete, final.
Hey, so, I guess we're—
Yeah. I heard.
...Cool. Cool, cool.
Alex doesn't answer. The pencil doesn't stop moving.
Details on the board. You've got the week.
The library's back table, the one nobody fights over. Alex has the notebook open — actually open, not angled away.
So. Identity map. We're supposed to do, like — symbols. Things that are 'us.'
Right. Yeah. I have zero symbols. I have a lot of moving boxes, if that counts.
...That could work, actually.
lines.emotion: small, almost a laugh, catches it, stops

lines.text: A pause. Alex's finger hovers over a page, then turns it — toward Wren, not away.
This is, um. This is the actual one. Not the — not what I said at the table that day. The real map.
...Whoa.
lines.action: leans in, careful not to touch the page

lines.text: It's got a real name too. Not the one you—
Yeah. I know.
If we're doing this. The project, whatever — you don't get to use my stuff as material. Not ever. Not even the safe version. Not even if it's funny.
lines.action: taps the desk — four times, steady

lines.text: I don't — I don't hand this to a lot of people.
Same table. Alex has a single index card, half-filled, angled so Wren can't quite see the rest.
So, what do you have so far?
Three symbols. That's the assignment. Three's enough.
lines.action: slides the card over, not the notebook

lines.text: ...This is really general. Like, 'likes to draw' general.
It's accurate.
I just think if we had more to work with—
Why should I trust you with anything else?
It's not rhetorical. Alex is actually waiting for an answer.
Presentation day. Wren and Alex's poster is propped against the front table, waiting.
Please tell me you're not actually presenting the doodle kingdom.
lines.action: leans over, voice pitched to carry

lines.emotion: laughs early, then catches Alex's face, stops

lines.text: Alex is close enough to hear every word. Doesn't move. Doesn't look up. Just — waits.
(There it is. Right there. Free.)
Alex double-checks the poster board for the third time — hands steady, everything else not quite.
Please tell me you're not actually presenting the doodle kingdom.
lines.emotion: smirk a little sharper this time

lines.text: Alex's hand stops on the poster. Doesn't turn around. Waiting to see which version of Wren shows up.
(Last time I let this exact laugh win. It's still sitting right there.)
Alex hasn't said more than four words to Wren all week. The poster's finished — technically a team effort, mostly Alex's handwriting.
Please tell me you're not actually presenting the doodle kingdom.
lines.action: not even bothering to lower her voice

lines.text: Alex doesn't wait to see what happens. Already stepping back, already pulling the notebook against their chest — the exact same posture as that first day.
...Right. Of course.
The hallway's emptying out, backpacks and noise fading toward the doors.
Hey.
lines.emotion: unusually — Alex catching up to someone

lines.text: Hey.
...That went okay. The presentation.
Yeah. It did.
Same time tomorrow? I want to show you the part I didn't present.
lines.action: taps the notebook, slower, less like armor now

lines.text: ...Yeah. Yeah, I'd like that.
Not everything. Enough to build on.
Alex doesn't linger, but doesn't rush off either — a kind of stillness Wren hasn't seen from them before.
(Not a yes. Not a no either. I can live with a maybe.)
...See you tomorrow.
See you tomorrow.
lines.emotion: careful not to sound too eager

lines.text: ...Same table.
lines.action: already walking, half-turned back to say it
lines.emotion: almost an afterthought, but not quite

lines.text: The door doesn't close. It's just left a little open — nothing promised, nothing decided. Whatever gets built from here is still Wren's to earn.
Maya's table again — but different now, easier, like Wren finally passed some invisible test.
Okay but you were kind of a legend in there yesterday, ngl.
Honestly? Immaculate. No for real.
...Thanks.
lines.emotion: smiling, but it doesn't sit right

lines.text: Two tables over, a chair sits empty. Alex isn't at it. Alex isn't anywhere Wren can see.
(It should feel better than this.)
Alex passes her in the hallway without a flicker of recognition. Not cold, exactly. Just — finished.
...Hey.
No response. The project got turned in, technically. A grade, some staples, nothing else.
(I keep thinking there'll be another chance. There isn't always another chance.)

options.text: Get up and go find Alex.
Stay, laugh along, fix it later.

a.text: Wren stands, chair scraping loud enough to turn heads.
Wren?
Oh — okay, yeah, go, we'll, um, save your seat—
Back in a sec.

b.action: forces a laugh, sits back into it

b.text: That's what I'm talking about.
(Fix it later. That's a plan. That's basically a plan.)

options.text: Name exactly what you did and why it was wrong — no excuses.
Apologize, but explain you were just trying to be funny.

a.text: I took something you showed me — in private, because you trusted me with it — and I turned it into a joke for a table full of people who don't even know your name. That was wrong. Not 'oops,' wrong. I did that.
...Yeah. You did.
a.action: long pause, taps the notebook again

a.text: Okay.
It's not forgiveness. But it's not a door slamming, either.

b.text: I'm sorry, I really am — I just, the table went quiet and I panicked and it just kind of came out, I wasn't trying to—
So it's an accident. Great. Feel better?
That's not what I—
It's fine.
Alex's shoulders don't unclench. If anything, they climb higher.

options.text: Push through and say something, even rushed.
Let it go — deal with it later.

a.text: Alex — wait—
Alex stops. Doesn't turn all the way around.
I shouldn't have said that. Any of it. I'm sorry — for real, not just, like, hallway-sorry.
...Okay.
Not much. But it's something, said out loud, before the door could close on it for good.

b.text: The door closes.
(Later. Definitely later. Not never — just, later.)
...Later.
The hallway empties. Later doesn't feel like a promise. It feels like an exit.

options.text: I hear you. That's fair — thank you for telling me.
I already said sorry, what more do you want from me?

a.text: ...Okay.
Okay. Good.
Something in Alex's shoulders finally, fully comes down.
This is the capital. It's called—
a.action: turns the page back toward Wren, testing a laugh before it happens

a.text: —it's called Cardboardia. I was eight. Don't.
I would never.

b.text: ...Nothing. Forget it.
The page is gone before Wren can say anything else.
Everything okay over here?
b.action: glancing over, not stepping in yet

b.text: Fine.
(That wasn't what fair sounded like. That was a door.)

options.text: Admit the truth: you wanted to matter at that table, and it cost you something you didn't mean to spend.
Deflect: 'It's not that big a deal, you're overreacting.'

a.text: ...Because I wanted to be funny. Wanted them to like me. And you were just — there. Easy. And it cost you something that wasn't mine to spend, and I knew that, even while I was doing it.
a.emotion: quiet, no jokes to hide behind

a.text: ...Huh.
a.action: long pause, taps notebook once, then stops

a.text: That's the first thing you've said all week that didn't sound like a bit.
It's not trust. But it's a crack of light under a closed door.

b.text: Right. Okay.
b.emotion: short, cold laugh, no humor in it

b.text: Alex doesn't ask again. The rest of the session happens in near-total silence — pencils, the clock, nothing else.
(That landed exactly like it deserved to.)

options.text: It's actually really good — you'll see.
Laugh it off vaguely to keep the peace with Maya's table.

a.text: It's actually really good. You'll see.
...Okay, damn, defensive.

a.action: glances over, quick, unreadable — then back to the poster

a.text: Wren and Alex, whenever you're ready.
a.action: from across the room, not looking up

a.text: Alex's hand, for just a second, stops tapping.

b.text: I mean — you'll see, I guess.
Right?
Alex's jaw tightens. The four taps on the table edge start again, faster this time.
(That wasn't a no. Alex heard that it wasn't a no.)

options.text: Hold the line, clearly and publicly.

a.text: It's not a joke, Maya. It's good. We worked on it all week.
a.emotion: loud enough for the row behind them too

a.text: ...Okay, okay, chill, I was just—
...Thanks.
a.emotion: something unguarded flickers, half a second

a.text: It's one word. It's enough.

b.text: It's not that bad, okay, calm down—
It's not a defense. It's barely a shrug wearing a defense's clothes.

b.action: turns back to the poster, says nothing

b.text: Whenever you two are ready.
Alex goes first. Alone, basically, even standing right next to her.

options.text: Stop everything and actually mean it this time — apologize again, in front of everyone if that's what it takes.
Freeze, say nothing, let the moment pass.

a.text: No — Maya, stop. Alex, wait—
a.emotion: voice cracking a little, but not stopping

a.text: I'm sorry. I know I've said it before and it didn't mean anything because I didn't back it up. I'm saying it in front of everyone this time because that's apparently what it takes, and I should've done it days ago.
The room's gone quiet in the specific way rooms go quiet when something real happens by accident.
...Okay.
a.action: long pause, doesn't fully unclench, but doesn't leave either

b.text: Wren opens her mouth. Nothing comes out in time.
Anyway—
Alex doesn't look back. Presents to the wall, mostly. Never once to Wren.
(That's twice now. Some doors only open so many times.)

lines.text: You know, you didn't have to say all that stuff about the map being good. In front of everyone. Maya's whole table.
I know. I wanted to, though. It's true.
...Nobody's really done that for me before. Just — said it, out loud, and meant it right away.
You know I almost didn't come find you. In the hallway. I figured you weren't going to.
I almost didn't come find you either. I let it sit way too long before I did.
Yeah. I noticed.
But you came back. That's the part I remember now, actually. Not the other part.
Whatever happened with you and the notebook kid anyway? You guys were like, a whole team there for a minute.
...It didn't really work out.
(I said the right words once. I just never figured out how to keep saying them.)
Honestly? Probably for the best, right? You're, like, back at the table.
...Right.
(I let the door close so many times I stopped noticing it was still a door.)
Somewhere back at the start, there was a hallway, and a bell, and a choice that felt small at the time.
It wasn't small. It just felt like it, until it wasn't.
(Later never came. I don't think I ever actually meant for it to.)

flavorLines.text: It's not the ending where everything gets fixed. It's the one where nothing gets ruined further — which, some days, is its own kind of win.
(Same table tomorrow. That's not nothing. That's a start.)

voiceNotes: Wren shields with speed and cleverness — she talks fastest right after she's said something true, like she's covering the sound of it. Her apologies should never be polished; the good ones are a little clumsy and specific, the bad ones are smooth and full of qualifiers ('I just,' 'I didn't mean,' 'I was trying to'). Maya never apologizes on-screen and never lingers on discomfort — her 'right?' is armor, and her flat, quiet register (rare) is the tell that something landed. Jordan's warmth is real but his jokes-to-fill-silence are a tell of his own anxiety; his laugh coming a half-second early or his smile faltering late are the two barometers for how much he's noticed. Alex's dialogue should almost always be shorter than everyone else's in a scene — clipped sentences that only lengthen when the notebook/kingdom is the actual subject. The four-tap motif on the desk/notebook is Alex's clearest tell: present and steady = guarded but managing; interrupted or sped-up = losing composure; absent = either full trust or total shutdown, and the scene should make clear which. Mr. Alvarez should almost never resolve a moment directly — he redirects with 'here's the thing' or a knuckle-tap and lets kids find their own way there; his one deviation (checking in during library-cold) is meant to feel like a small, deliberate crack in his usual restraint.

alternativeLines.reasoning: 'The Doodle Kingdom' ties directly to the episode title and reads as the most natural, most quotable version of the joke — keeping the alternates in reserve in case a lighter or sillier register is needed.

alternatives: Cool. So I overreacted. Got it.
So that makes it okay, then?
alternativeLines.reasoning: Alex's clipped deadpan can flex slightly colder or slightly more wounded depending on how guarded the performance should read in this branch.

alternatives: I'm sorry. For real this time — not the hallway version, not the quick version. I should've meant it the first time.
I keep saying sorry and not backing it up. I'm backing it up now, in front of everyone, because that's apparently what it takes.
alternativeLines.reasoning: This is the highest-stakes apology line in the episode; alternates offer slightly different balances of vulnerability vs. directness depending on how raw the fractured-branch performance should feel.
